A powerful low passing far north of the islands produced surf of 20 to 35 feet along the north facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 10 to 20 feet along the west facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, and the Big Island of Hawaii; and 6 to 12 feet along the east facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, and the Big Island.  The hazardous surf kept lifeguards busy on these isles with assisting and rescuing swimmers and surfers.  Water coming over the pavement closed a portion of Bayfront Highway in Hilo on the Big Island for a time on the 16th.  Water was crossing other shoreline roadways on these islands during this episode as well, though no significant disruption to traffic flow was noted.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.

A powerful low passing far north of the islands produced surf of 20 to 35 feet along the north facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 10 to 20 feet along the west facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, and the Big Island of Hawaii; and 6 to 12 feet along the east facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, and the Big Island. Water coming over the pavement closed a portion of Bayfront Highway in Hilo on the Big Island for a time on the 16th.  Water was crossing other shoreline roadways on these islands during this episode as well, though no significant disruption to traffic flow was noted.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.

A surface low northwest of the Garden Island of Kauai, a surface front, and an upper air trough generated heavy showers and thunderstorms over most of the Aloha State.  Several schools were closed, and many roadways became impassable because of high water and debris.  Damages were extensive to homes and other structures, and to agriculture.  The cost estimate for the losses was $50 million.  There were no serious injuries, though a teenage boy suffered a leg cut before being rescued from flood waters near Waianae on Oahu.

No serious property damage or injuries occurred, even though lifeguards kept busy aiding distressed swimmers and surfers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low far west/northwest of the state produced surf of 10 to 15 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and 6 to 10 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii. Heavy showers and thunderstorms caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding in windward coastal and windward upslope sections of East Maui.  An upper trough near the islands helped enhance trade showers.  No significant injuries or property damage were reported.

An upper low near the Aloha State helped generate showers and thunderstorms, and snow at the higher elevations, over Maui and the Big Island of Hawaii.  The system also helped generate a severe thunderstorm on the Big Island.  The rain events caused occasional problems, as debris flows shut down parts of roadways on the Big Island on the 29th and 30th. No serious property damage or injuries were reported, however.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An airline pilot reported a hail stone of 0.75 of an inch about 1130 Hawaii Standard Time on the 29th around 5 miles north of Kailua-Kona on the Big Island of Hawaii.

A surface low northwest of the Garden Island of Kauai, a surface front, and an upper air trough generated heavy showers and thunderstorms over most of the Aloha State.  Several schools were closed, and many roadways became impassable because of high water and debris.  Damages were extensive to homes and other structures, and to agriculture.  The cost estimate for the losses was $50 million.  There were no serious injuries, though a teenage boy suffered a leg cut before being rescued from flood waters near Waianae on Oahu.

An upper trough over the Hawaiian Islands enhanced trade wind showers, which resulted in wintry conditions in the higher elevations, and heavy rain, thunderstorms, and flash flooding over much of the state at lower elevations.  More than 200 homes, most of them on the Big Island of Hawaii and in Kauai, were damaged by the high water.  Because of high water and landslides, the flooding closed many roadways from time to time.  At least two cars and a 40-foot shipping container were swept into a stream and river in Kauai on the 3rd of February, with the container hitting two bridges as it moved down the Hanapepe River to the ocean.  No dollar cost estimate on damages was available.  There were no serious injuries.

A fire burned 95 acres of brush in the West Maui State Forest Reserve near  3200 feet elevation.  Initially, gusty winds around 30 to 40 mph hampered firefighters in battling the blaze.  Later on, however, winds decreased and the fire was brought under control.  The fire had threatened native plants and animals.  The cause of the blaze was unknown, and damage estimates were not available.  There were no reports of serious injuries.
A portion of Oahu, that includes Waimanalo, and West Molokai were added to the Drought Monitor at the D2 level (severe drought).  Poor rainfall led to water restrictions in both areas.

A subtropical low - known as a Kona low in Hawaii - caused heavy rain and showers over Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, and Molokai.  The precipitation produced small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding of roadways.  No significant property damage or injuries were reported.

A power line, probably downed by high winds, ignited a fire on the isle of Lanai that blackened 1000 acres of brush.  The blaze forced county officials to evacuate about 600 residents and guests of the Four Seasons Resort at Manele Bay for a brief time as a precaution.  The fire persisted for four days because of dry conditions and gusty winds. There were no serious injuries.  Damage estimates were not available.

Heavy rain caused flash flooding near Kapaa and Wailua, Kauai.  Deep ponding on road surfaces forced the closure of Kawaihau Road at the Kahuna Road junction in Kapaa; and Kamalu Road near Kalama Stream in the vicinity of Wailua.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.

Heavy showers over windward sections of Oahu produced ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  There were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.

A deep low far north of the area generated surf of 12 to 18 feet along the north-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 8 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai.||Three individuals died as a direct result from the high surf.  Two females, ages 33 and 38, were swept out to sea when a large wave struck a rocky shoreline at Queen's Bath on the north shore of Kauai in the afternoon of the 12th.  A male drowned while swimming at Hanakapiai Beach on Kauai's north shore in the morning of the 13th.  There were no other reports of serious injuries or property damage resulting from the high surf episode.

A surface low northwest of the Garden Island of Kauai, a surface front, and an upper air trough generated heavy showers and thunderstorms over most of the Aloha State.  Several schools were closed, and many roadways became impassable because of high water and debris.  Damages were extensive to homes and other structures, and to agriculture.  The cost estimate for the losses was $50 million.  There were no serious injuries, though a teenage boy suffered a leg cut before being rescued from flood waters near Waianae on Oahu.
